findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `elif cov.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict['verdict'] = 'INDETERMINATE'` to `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` (alongside the `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` branches) so the post-drop recompute respects the new flag — enumerate this as a co-edit target in the plan.
evidence: `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` docstring: 'Re-derive the verdict STRING after the drift floor drops findings, using the SAME rule as orchestrator.finalize_verdict'
The function checks `cov.get('llm_unavailable')` and `cov.get('verify_failed')` but has no `hierarchy_incomplete` branch
Plan Remediation step 3 names only `finalize_verdict` as the edit target for the new `elif` branch
Plan AC line 3 says 'finalize_verdict gains a new elif ...' — no mention of `_recompute_verdict_after_drop`

Scenario: hierarchy read fails → `hierarchy_incomplete=True` → `finalize_verdict` sets INDETERMINATE correctly → drift floor later drops a BLOCK finding → `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` runs, sees no `blocking`, no `llm_unavailable`, no `verify_failed` → sets verdict to `PASS`
findings.finding: `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` is an unenumerated co-edit target: it mirrors `finalize_verdict`'s verdict-recomputation rule verbatim (confirmed by its own docstring) and checks `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` but will NOT check `hierarchy_incomplete`. After any drift/xcheck floor drops a BLOCK finding post-`finalize_verdict`, this function re-derives the verdict from scratch and can flip it to `PASS` even when `hierarchy_incomplete` is set — bypassing the new INDETERMINATE guard. It is called from three sites: `drift_floor.apply_to_verdict` (line 272), `xcheck.apply_contradictions` (line 180), and `xcheck.apply_comment_trail` (line 311).
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/drift_floor.py:190-211 (`_recompute_verdict_after_drop`); also xcheck.py:180, xcheck.py:311
scenarios: A container ticket with a failed child enumeration gets INDETERMINATE from finalize_verdict, then a drift/xcheck floor drops its only BLOCK finding; _recompute_verdict_after_drop then returns PASS, nullifying the INDETERMINATE.
findings.suggested_fix: In `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop`, after the `elif cov.get('verify_failed'):` block and before the final `else: verdict['verdict'] = 'PASS'`, add: `elif cov.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict['verdict'] = 'INDETERMINATE'`. Mirror this in the plan's Remediation and AC so all three call sites (drift_floor.py:272, xcheck.py:180, xcheck.py:311) inherit the fix through the shared function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` in `drift_floor.py` to the plan's remediation scope: specify that the same `elif coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` branch must be added there alongside `finalize_verdict`, keeping the two copies in sync.
evidence: drift_floor.py lines 190-211: _recompute_verdict_after_drop mirrors finalize_verdict's elif chain (llm_unavailable → INDETERMINATE; verify_failed → conditional; else → PASS) with no mention of hierarchy_incomplete
The plan's Remediation step 3 and all AC items reference only finalize_verdict — _recompute_verdict_after_drop is never mentioned

drift_floor.py line 191 comment: 'Re-derive the verdict STRING after the drift floor drops findings, using the SAME rule as orchestrator.finalize_verdict' — explicitly a parallel copy that must stay in sync
findings.finding: `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` (orchestrator.py:503 parallel copy at drift_floor.py:190-211) replicates `finalize_verdict`'s coverage-flag if/elif chain verbatim but is not mentioned anywhere in the plan's remediation. Adding `elif coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` to `finalize_verdict` without adding the matching branch to `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` means a verdict that was downgraded from BLOCK by the drift floor will re-derive to PASS (the else branch at line 211) even when `hierarchy_incomplete` is set — a silent bypass of the new guard in exactly the convergent re-review path.
findings.location: Remediation step 3 / AC item 3: specifies only `finalize_verdict`; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/drift_floor.py lines 190-211
scenarios: A container ticket with incomplete hierarchy reads (hierarchy_incomplete=True) is re-reviewed after code drift. The drift floor drops a novel blocking finding, triggering _recompute_verdict_after_drop. Because hierarchy_incomplete has no branch there, the function falls to the else branch and sets verdict='PASS' — despite the incomplete hierarchy, producing a hollow PASS that finalize_verdict would have blocked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Remediation step 3: 'Also add the matching `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` branch to `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` (drift_floor.py:190), which mirrors `finalize_verdict`'s branch logic and must stay in sync with it.' Add a corresponding AC item: 'drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop also routes to INDETERMINATE when hierarchy_incomplete is set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit whether `_reads.list_tickets`/`_reads.show_ticket` can actually fail transiently (they read local git objects, not network endpoints); if not, replace 'bounded retries' with the accurate existing pattern — degrade-with-logging on first failure + set `hierarchy_incomplete=True` — matching `_assemble_context_uncached`'s own existing catch blocks (orchestrator.py:175-187), and remove the citation of verify.py's chunk loop as a retry precedent since it is a degrade pattern, not a retry pattern.
evidence: Plan: 'bounded retry (matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop)'
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:315-334 — chunk loop catches exceptions and appends `[]`, no re-attempt (degrade, not retry)
Search for 'retry' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero hits in orchestrator.py/passes.py/pass1.py — no existing retry pattern in this subpackage
Retry infrastructure: src/rebar/llm/anthropic_model.py:90-153 (Tenacity HTTP transport), src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/dispatch_one.py:113-166 — all network-layer, not local git reads
findings.finding: The plan instructs implementers to add 'bounded retries' for `_reads.list_tickets` / `_reads.show_ticket` failures and explicitly cites 'verify.py's chunk retry loop' as the matching existing pattern. Examination shows verify.py does NOT retry — it degrades silently (catch → append `[]` → continue, with no re-attempt). No existing retry pattern for synchronous local git reads exists anywhere in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`. All actual retry infrastructure in the codebase is Tenacity-based at the HTTP transport layer (`anthropic_model.py`, `dispatch_one.py`). Citing a degrade-not-retry pattern as the prior art for building a retry loop is cargo-cult specification: the implementer would build new infrastructure unsupported by the cited precedent, for a read layer (local git orphan-branch, synchronous) whose failure mode as transient/retriable is not documented.
findings.location: Remediation step 2 / AC line 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries (matching this package's existing retry pattern)'
scenarios: An implementer follows the plan literally, looks at verify.py, sees a degrade-not-retry loop, and either implements the wrong thing (a degrade dressed up as a retry) or builds a novel Tenacity wrapper for local git reads — neither of which is grounded in a genuine existing pattern.

Transient failure evidence is absent: local git reads fail for structural reasons (missing file, corrupt object), not transiently — retrying them would mask real errors rather than recovering from network blips.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC2's retry language with: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures set `hierarchy_incomplete=True` immediately (matching `_assemble_context_uncached`'s existing broad-except-with-logging pattern at orchestrator.py:175-187); the bounded-retry language and the verify.py citation are dropped since local git reads do not fail transiently and no retry pattern for this layer exists in the package.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the reference to 'verify.py's chunk retry loop' in Remediation §2 / AC#2 with a real existing retry exemplar in the codebase, or specify the bounded-retry implementation concretely (e.g. N attempts with exponential backoff before setting hierarchy_incomplete=True), since verify.py uses degrade-not-retry.
evidence: Plan text: 'recoverable child-list/child-fetch errors get a small bounded retry (matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop)'

verify.py lines 315-334: `for chunk in chunks: try: raw_output = list(run_chunk(...)) except StructuredOutputError: ... chunk_outputs.append([]); continue  except Exception: chunk_outputs.append([]); continue` — failure degrades silently, no retry.
Search for 'retry' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review returned only unrelated hits (resign.py comment, criteria_routing.json). No retry loop exists in verify.py or anywhere in the plan_review package.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'verify.py's chunk retry loop' as the reference retry pattern to match when implementing bounded retries for hierarchy reads, but verify.py contains no retry loop. Its verify_findings() function iterates chunks and DEGRADES on failure (appends [] and continues with the next chunk) — it never retries a failed chunk. The cited exemplar does not exist, leaving AC#2 ('bounded retries matching this package's existing retry pattern') without a concrete reference implementation.
findings.location: Remediation §2 / AC line 2: 'bounded retries (matching this package's existing retry pattern, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop)'
scenarios: An implementor following the spec looks for the retry pattern in verify.py, finds only a degrade-on-failure loop, and either implements a degrade (violating AC#2's intent) or is blocked needing to invent a retry pattern with no existing reference.
If the intent is to match the degrade pattern (not actually retry), AC#2 as written is misleading — 'bounded retries' and 'degrade on failure' are opposite behaviors.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) identify a real retry pattern in the package to reference (search the broader rebar codebase for a bounded retry helper), or (b) specify the retry directly: 'attempt child-list/child-fetch up to N times (e.g. 3) with brief back-off; on exhaustion, set hierarchy_incomplete=True' — removing the false verify.py reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or replace the 'verify.py chunk retry loop' analogy: identify the actual retry pattern for synchronous `_reads` store calls (or acknowledge there is none and specify what mechanism/exception-types/retry-count the implementer should use), since verify.py's bounded retry targets LLM structured-output calls, not filesystem/git ticket-store reads.
evidence: Plan text: 'recoverable child-list/child-fetch errors ... get a small bounded retry (matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop)'
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:302: 'bounded retry' refers to StructuredOutputError on LLM runs, not store reads
src/rebar/_reads.py:53-62: show_ticket maps ReadError → RebarError with no retry envelope
No retry infrastructure found in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ for _reads calls (search confirmed)
findings.finding: The plan directs implementers to model bounded retries on 'verify.py's chunk retry loop', but that loop retries LLM structured-output calls (StructuredOutputError on model invocations), not synchronous filesystem/git-based ticket store reads. The _reads.py facade has no retry infrastructure for store reads — it maps ReadError directly to RebarError with no retry. There is no existing analogous pattern in the package to 'match', so the claim is an unverified capability assertion that will mislead implementation.
findings.location: Remediation §2 — 'Bounded retries with actionable diagnostics'; AC2 — 'matching this package's existing retry pattern'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan looks for a retry scaffold in verify.py or plan_review/ for store reads and finds none — they must build one from scratch, requiring unspecified decisions about mechanism (tenacity vs. bare loop), exception types (RebarError? ReadError? OSError?), and retry-count source.

If the implementer assumes the LLM transport retry (tenacity/anthropic_model.py) is applicable and wires it to store reads, the retry semantics will be wrong (it retries on HTTP 429/5xx, not filesystem errors).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the analogy with a concrete specification: e.g. 'use a bare `for attempt in range(N)` loop catching `RebarError` (and optionally `OSError`) on `_reads.list_tickets` / `_reads.show_ticket`, with N=3 and no backoff (these are local file reads, not network calls); there is no existing store-read retry scaffold to match — this is new code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the phantom 'verify.py chunk retry loop' reference with the actual existing retry exemplar in the package (e.g. the transport-layer tenacity pattern in `anthropic_model.py`, or a simple bounded `for attempt in range(N)` loop if no ticket-store retry precedent exists), or specify the retry contract (max attempts, backoff, exception types) directly in the plan.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py lines 315-334: for chunk in chunks: try: raw_output = ... except StructuredOutputError: ... continue; except Exception: chunk_outputs.append([]); continue — no retry, pure degrade
grep for 'retry' in plan_review/ returns no hits for any loop construct in verify.py
The actual retry infrastructure is in src/rebar/llm/anthropic_model.py:_build_retrying_anthropic_model (transport-layer tenacity on HTTP calls, not ticket-store reads)
findings.finding: The plan cites 'verify.py's chunk retry loop' as the existing retry pattern to match for bounded ticket-store retries, but no such loop exists in verify.py. The verify path degrades silently on exception with no retry (lines 315-334 of verify.py show bare except → continue with no retry). The only retry infrastructure in the package is a transport-layer tenacity wrapper for LLM HTTP calls (anthropic_model.py/_build_retrying_anthropic_model), which is irrelevant to ticket-store reads. An implementer following this phantom reference has no actual pattern to copy.
findings.location: Remediation step 2: 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop'
scenarios: An implementer searches verify.py for the 'chunk retry loop' to model the bounded-retry logic and finds nothing — either they skip retries entirely (violating AC2) or invent an ad-hoc pattern inconsistent with the rest of the package.

An implementer copies the transport-layer tenacity retry (anthropic_model.py) thinking that is the referenced pattern, producing an overengineered or incorrectly-scoped retry for synchronous ticket-store reads.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation step 2, replace 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop' with a self-contained retry specification: 'a small bounded retry (e.g. up to 3 attempts with no backoff, catching the same exception classes as the enumeration/fetch try/except blocks in _assemble_context_uncached) before falling back'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `verify.py` chunk-retry analogy with an accurate reference: either cite the actual tenacity transport-retry config (`LLMConfig.llm_retry_max_attempts`) if ticket-store reads should use the same tenacity wrapper, or acknowledge there is no existing store-read retry and specify the retry mechanism explicitly (e.g., a simple `for attempt in range(MAX_ATTEMPTS)` loop with exponential backoff, or a tenacity `@retry` decorator matching the transport config's stop/wait parameters).
evidence: Plan text: 'recoverable child-list/child-fetch errors … get a small bounded retry (matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. `verify.py`'s chunk retry loop)'

review_kernel/verify.py:290-334 — verify.py's 'chunk retry loop' is: try run_chunk → except StructuredOutputError (LLM schema validation failure) → continue/drop, NOT a ticket-store I/O retry with sleep/backoff
runner.py:510-582 — the single bounded structured-output retry is an LLM reask, not a store read retry
orchestrator.py:173-187 — existing _reads.show_ticket/_reads.list_tickets calls have no retry wrapper; ABSENCE of any ticket-store retry pattern in this package
findings.finding: The plan's claimed retry-pattern analogy — 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. `verify.py`'s chunk retry loop' — does not describe a ticket-store I/O retry. `verify.py`'s 'chunk retry loop' is a bounded LLM structured-output reask: a single LLM call whose response fails schema validation gets one reask with the validation error fed back. There is no existing bounded-retry pattern for synchronous `_reads.show_ticket` / `_reads.list_tickets` calls in this package; the LLM transport retry (tenacity/httpx) is also unrelated. Citing a non-existent analogy as the reference implementation leaves AC2 ('bounded retries matching this package's existing retry pattern') unanchored, and an implementer may either copy the wrong retry mechanism (an LLM reask loop around a ticket read) or be unable to identify what 'matching' means.
findings.location: Remediation step 2 / AC2 — 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. `verify.py`'s chunk retry loop'
scenarios: Implementer searches for 'verify.py chunk retry loop', finds the LLM structured-output reask, and implements a reask-style loop around `_reads.show_ticket` — a nonsensical pattern for a synchronous Python function call.
Implementer cannot identify a matching retry pattern and either adds no retry (violating AC2) or introduces an ad-hoc pattern inconsistent with the rest of the package.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify precisely where `hierarchy_incomplete` lives (on `PlanContext` as a field, in `coverage` as a key set during assembly, or both) and, if on `PlanContext`, name the exact orchestration callsite that transfers `ctx.hierarchy_incomplete` into the `coverage` dict before `finalize_verdict` is invoked.
evidence: Plan text: 'New `PlanContext`/coverage field: `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]: bool`' — uses slash notation treating them as one entity
orchestrator.py:503-541 — `finalize_verdict` reads only the `coverage` dict argument, never `ctx` for failure flags
orchestrator.py:163-211 — `_assemble_context_uncached` returns a `PlanContext`; `coverage` is a separate dict assembled downstream by the workflow ops
orchestrator.py:108-123 — `assemble_context_cache` memoizes the `PlanContext`; memoized result is shared across workflow steps that each build their own `coverage` dicts
findings.finding: The plan conflates `PlanContext` fields and `coverage` dict keys as if they are the same storage location ('new `PlanContext`/coverage field'), but they are distinct objects: `_assemble_context_uncached` builds and caches a `PlanContext`, while `coverage` is a separate dict assembled by the workflow ops and passed into `finalize_verdict`. `finalize_verdict` reads `coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` — NOT a `PlanContext` attribute. If `hierarchy_incomplete` is stored only as a `PlanContext` field, the flag must be explicitly transferred to `coverage` somewhere in the orchestration path before `finalize_verdict` is called; the plan never specifies where this transfer occurs, leaving a real implementation gap that could result in the flag being set on the context but never reaching verdict routing.
findings.location: Remediation step 1 / AC1 — 'new `PlanContext`/coverage field'
scenarios: Implementer adds `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` to `PlanContext` and sets it during assembly, but never writes it into `coverage`; `finalize_verdict` sees `coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` as falsy and returns PASS for an incomplete-hierarchy container review.

Implementer sets `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete'] = True` only inside `run_pass1` or a workflow op, but the workflow step that calls `finalize_verdict` uses a different `coverage` dict instance; the flag is lost.
findings.suggested_fix: The cleanest fit with the existing pattern: (a) add `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` to `PlanContext`; (b) at the start of `finalize_verdict` (or in the workflow op that calls it), write `if ctx.hierarchy_incomplete: coverage['hierarchy_incomplete'] = True; coverage['hierarchy_incomplete_detail'] = ctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail` — making the transfer explicit and testable. Alternatively, set `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete']` directly during assembly by accepting a mutable `coverage` dict parameter, matching how other assembly steps populate coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the bounded retry for child-list/child-fetch is a new store-read retry pattern (not a copy of verify.py's LLM contract retry), and specify the intended exception types and retry count (e.g. transient IOError/network failures, N=3 attempts) so implementers have unambiguous guidance.
evidence: Plan Remediation step 2: 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop'
verify.py:302: 'even after json-repair + the bounded retry — a divergent SHAPE' — confirms the retry is for LLM structured-output contract failures, not store reads
No existing bounded store-read retry found anywhere in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` via search
findings.finding: The plan's retry-pattern analogy ('matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. `verify.py`'s chunk retry loop') points to `review_kernel/verify.py`, which implements LLM structured-output retries (catching `StructuredOutputError` from the model), not bounded ticket-store read retries. There is no existing ticket-store read retry in `plan_review/` to match, so the 'matching' reference is ambiguous for implementers — the new bounded retry for `_reads.list_tickets`/`_reads.show_ticket` is a net-new pattern in this layer.
findings.location: Remediation step 2; `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:302`
scenarios: An implementer looks for the retry pattern in verify.py and finds LLM-specific retry logic (StructuredOutputError, json-repair) that does not apply to ticket-store reads, leading to confusion about what 'matching' means for backoff policy, exception types, and retry count.

findings.finding: The retry protocol for recoverable child-list/fetch failures is referenced but its parameters (retry count, backoff, placement in the call stack) are never specified, leaving the AC unimplementable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2; orchestrator.py:173-187 (_assemble_context_uncached child enumeration)

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact creation/initialisation site for `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]` (default `False`) and `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` (default `[]`), naming the function or dict-construction point that owns them — consistent with how existing coverage flags are initialised — so the full CREATE path is explicit.
evidence: Plan: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]: bool — True when child enumeration or per-child detail loading did not complete successfully after bounded retries'
Plan does not name a specific function, class method, or initialisation site responsible for creating these keys — only says 'set coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = True' on the failure path.

The existing pattern (llm_unavailable, verify_failed) presumably initialises these keys to False at coverage-dict creation time, but the plan does not instruct the implementer to follow that same initialisation convention for the new flag.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which function owns the creation of `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]` / `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]`, nor at what point in the orchestrator's pipeline these keys must be present. Without an explicit creation point, an implementer may initialise them too late (e.g. only on the failure branch, never initialising to False/[] on the success path), leaving `finalize_verdict`'s `coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete")` to silently return None (falsy) even when the key was never set — obscuring whether the reads succeeded or were simply never attempted.
findings.location: Remediation §1–2 (new PlanContext/coverage field) and AC bullet 1: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]: bool … distinct from both has_children and llm_unavailable'
scenarios: An implementer who only writes `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete'] = True` on the failure branch and never initialises it to `False` on success: finalize_verdict's `coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` returns None on a successful hierarchy read — indistinguishable from False, so verdict routing is correct by accident, but an operator or test inspecting `coverage` cannot tell whether the key is absent because hierarchy was complete or because the initialisation was forgotten.
findings.suggested_fix: In the coverage-dict initialisation block (wherever `llm_unavailable` and `verify_failed` are initialised to their defaults), add `'hierarchy_incomplete': False, 'hierarchy_incomplete_detail': []` so the keys are always present and consumers can rely on their presence without `.get()` fallback ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete retry default in the plan (e.g. 'up to 3 attempts with 1-second linear back-off, matching verify.py's chunk retry loop') so the executor does not need to read a separate file to determine the bound.
evidence: 'recoverable child-list/child-fetch errors…get a small bounded retry (matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop)'
No retry count, backoff interval, or 'matching means same count' statement appears anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The retry bound is delegated to the executor via 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop' without stating the actual count, delay, or backoff policy. An executor must read verify.py and infer what 'matching' means — the plan defers a concrete decision it should make.
findings.location: Remediation §2 / AC line: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries (matching this package's existing retry pattern)'
scenarios: Executor implements 1 retry; verify.py uses 3 — the two are now inconsistent and a reviewer cannot determine which is correct.
Executor interprets 'matching' as same mechanism (exponential) but uses a different count, passing review but differing from the referenced pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'matching the retry pattern already used elsewhere in this package, e.g. verify.py's chunk retry loop' with the explicit values, e.g.: 'up to 3 retries with 1-second delay between attempts (the same count and interval used in verify.py's chunk retry loop — state the values explicitly here so this spec is self-contained).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instrument tracker read failures (at retry exhaustion) with at least a structured log entry (e.g. `logger.warning("hierarchy_incomplete", detail=...)`) and, if metrics infrastructure is already in use in this package, a counter increment — so operators can detect and alert on degraded hierarchy coverage without inspecting individual verdict payloads.
evidence: 'record which specific reads failed or were omitted (e.g. coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: list[str])' — stored only in the coverage dict, not in a log/metric/trace
No mention of logging, metric emission, or alerting on retry exhaustion anywhere in the plan

The cascade-flag note acknowledges operator impact ('preserve actionable diagnostics so an operator can distinguish transient/retry from genuinely broken') but relies solely on the coverage dict for that signal
findings.finding: The plan adds new failure points (child-list and child-fetch reads against the tracker store) and routes them through the bounded-retry path, but does not specify that these failures are instrumented with a log entry, metric, or alert — only a dict field in the coverage payload is recorded.
findings.location: Remediation §2 / Acceptance Criteria (AC line: 'record which specific reads failed or were omitted')
scenarios: Tracker reads silently fail repeatedly in production; the coverage dict is only visible to whoever inspects the returned verdict, not to an on-call engineer watching logs or dashboards.
Retry exhaustion (a signal of a degraded dependency) goes undetected until a downstream consumer notices INDETERMINATE verdicts accumulating.
findings.suggested_fix: At the point where bounded retries are exhausted and `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = True` is set, emit a structured log warning: `logger.warning("plan_review.hierarchy_incomplete", ticket=<id>, failed_reads=coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"])`. If the package already emits StatsD/Prometheus counters elsewhere, add `metrics.increment("plan_review.hierarchy_incomplete")`.

findings.finding: The term 'recoverable' (distinguishing which failures get retries) is undefined — an executor cannot determine without investigation which error types qualify.
findings.location: AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC2: 'Recoverable = transient I/O failures (e.g., `OSError`, `asyncio.TimeoutError`); non-recoverable = permission, not-found, or parse errors which skip to incomplete-context result immediately.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test scenario for retry exhaustion: all bounded retries fail → `hierarchy_incomplete=True` is set (distinct from 'total child-list failure' which may not exercise the retry loop); verify that the flag is NOT set after fewer than the configured retry count.
evidence: AC final bullet lists: 'total child-list failure, one-child detail failure among several, retry-then-recovery (no hierarchy_incomplete set), and that finalize_verdict returns INDETERMINATE ...'
Remediation item 2 states: 'recoverable child-list/child-fetch errors get a small bounded retry ... before falling back; on exhaustion, set coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = True'
No test scenario explicitly states 'all retries exhausted → hierarchy_incomplete=True' to distinguish from an immediate (no-retry) failure
findings.finding: The retry-exhaustion failure path — where bounded retries are attempted and ALL fail, resulting in `hierarchy_incomplete=True` — is not explicitly listed as a test scenario. The AC covers 'retry-then-recovery' (success after retry) but not 'retry-then-exhaustion' (all retries fail → flag set). This is the critical code path that gates the INDETERMINATE verdict for transient failures and is distinct from 'total child-list failure' (which may be interpreted as an immediate/first-call failure with no retry attempted).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet (test scenarios list)
scenarios: A transient tracker error occurs on every retry attempt; the retry loop exhausts; the test should confirm that (a) retries were attempted and (b) hierarchy_incomplete is set only after exhaustion — not on first failure
Without this test, a naive implementation that sets hierarchy_incomplete on the first error (skipping retries entirely) would pass all listed test cases
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC test-scenarios list: 'retry-then-exhaustion: a child-list or child-fetch error recurs across all configured retry attempts → hierarchy_incomplete=True is set and hierarchy_incomplete_detail records the failed read(s); confirm the implementation does not short-circuit to hierarchy_incomplete before exhausting retries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between leaf AC4 ('no relevance exception, unconditionally INDETERMINATE') and parent epic AC4's explicit 'unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant' carve-out: either (a) update the parent's AC4 to drop the carve-out first (which stales the parent's plan-review attestation), then re-review the leaf, or (b) realign the leaf to honour the parent's carve-out.
evidence: Parent AC4: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.'
Leaf Remediation §4: 'The originally proposed
deterministic
tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant
carve-out is **dropped**'
Leaf AC4: 'There is no relevance exception — incomplete hierarchy is unconditionally INDETERMINATE.'
The leaf does not narrow the parent's conditional — it removes the condition entirely, producing a stricter-than-authorized outcome contract.
findings.finding: The leaf explicitly drops the 'demonstrably irrelevant' carve-out that the parent epic's AC4 requires, directly contradicting the parent's acceptance criterion rather than narrowing it.
findings.location: Leaf Remediation §4 ('No relevance exception') and leaf AC4 ('There is no relevance exception — incomplete hierarchy is unconditionally INDETERMINATE') vs. parent epic AC4 ('Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.')
scenarios: An implementer reads the leaf and builds unconditional INDETERMINATE routing; the parent's AC4 still permits a relevance-exception path, leaving the parent's acceptance criterion unsatisfied.
A reviewer validating the leaf against the parent finds a direct contradiction, preventing the leaf from passing G7 containment.
findings.suggested_fix: If the technical argument in Remediation §4 (that the carve-out is unsafe) is accepted, amend parent AC4 to read: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely.' — remove the 'unless…' clause — then re-review the parent, and only then re-review this leaf. Do not silently diverge the leaf from the parent's current AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC group (e.g. a `pytest tests/plan_review/test_hierarchy_completeness.py -v` invocation for AC6, and a `grep -n 'hierarchy_incomplete' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` or equivalent for AC1/AC3/AC5) so done-ness is independently verifiable.
evidence: AC6 names test scenarios ('Tests cover: total child-list failure, one-child detail failure among several, retry-then-recovery…') but gives no file path or `pytest` invocation.
AC1–AC5 describe structural/behavioral outcomes but are not linked to any test, grep, or inspection command that would constitute evidence of completion.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion supplies a concrete proving command (test file path, pytest invocation, or inspection command). Test scenarios are described in AC6, but there is no stated command an agent can run to produce evidence that each AC is satisfied. AC1–AC5 in particular have no test reference at all.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, all six ACs
scenarios: An executor writes and passes all tests but places them in a non-standard location; a reviewer has no specified command to verify them.
AC3 ('finalize_verdict gains a new elif branch') is verifiable by code inspection, but no grep or AST check is specified — a reviewer must infer how to check it.
findings.suggested_fix: Append a '## Verification' section listing: (1) `pytest tests/llm/plan_review/test_hierarchy_completeness.py -v` covering AC6 scenarios; (2) `grep -c 'hierarchy_incomplete' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` asserting ≥3 occurrences for AC1/AC3/AC5; (3) `grep -n 'hierarchy_incomplete_detail' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` asserting the detail field is populated for AC5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Testing' subsection to the Remediation body enumerating the four test scenarios named in AC6 (total child-list failure, one-child detail failure, retry-then-recovery, and finalize_verdict INDETERMINATE regardless of other flags) so AC6 has a corresponding described deliverable.
evidence: Remediation items 1–4 describe: a new coverage field, bounded retries with diagnostics, a new finalize_verdict branch, and the no-exception policy. None mention test authorship or test scenarios.

AC6: 'Tests cover: total child-list failure, one-child detail failure among several, retry-then-recovery (no `hierarchy_incomplete` set), and that `finalize_verdict` returns `INDETERMINATE` (not `PASS`) whenever `hierarchy_incomplete` is set regardless of what findings/coverage flags are otherwise present.'
ABSENCE: No 'Testing' or 'Test plan' subsection exists anywhere in the plan body.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies a concrete test-deliverable (four named test scenarios) with no corresponding description of test work in the Remediation body — the Remediation section describes only runtime behavior changes (field, retries, branch, policy) and never mentions tests, leaving AC6 as an orphan criterion with no grounding in described work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6; Remediation section (all four numbered items)
scenarios: An implementer reads the Remediation body, ships the runtime changes, and skips tests because no test work was described — AC6 then fails at review with no prior warning.
A reviewer treating ACs as the authoritative checklist is surprised to find test scenarios that were never scoped in the work description.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a numbered item 5 in Remediation: 'Tests: add/extend unit tests covering (a) total child-list enumeration failure → hierarchy_incomplete=True, (b) one-child detail failure among several → hierarchy_incomplete=True with detail, (c) retry-then-recovery → no hierarchy_incomplete set, (d) finalize_verdict returns INDETERMINATE whenever hierarchy_incomplete is set, independent of all other coverage flags.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for the finalize_verdict behavioral change — e.g. a feature flag to revert to pre-change behavior, or documented steps to revert the elif branch — so the breaking change is not merely acknowledged but reversible.
evidence: Cascade flag: 'tightening failure behavior can affect claim-gate availability during transient tracker failures; bounded retries + the new coverage detail field preserve actionable diagnostics so an operator can distinguish "transient, retry" from "genuinely broken hierarchy read."'

Remediation #3: 'a remaining incomplete hierarchy can never produce an ordinary clean PASS' — this is a behavioral breaking change for any consumer that previously acted on PASS verdicts during degraded-hierarchy conditions.
ABSENCE: No expand-contract sequence, feature flag, rollback step, or back-out plan is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan changes finalize_verdict's output behavior — transient tracker failures that previously yielded a non-INDETERMINATE verdict will now yield INDETERMINATE — and acknowledges this downstream impact only in the 'Cascade flag' note, but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequencing for consumers (e.g. the claim-gate) that depend on the prior behavior.
findings.location: Cascade flag paragraph; Remediation item 3; Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: A transient tracker outage causes child enumeration to fail; claim-gate consumers that previously received PASS now receive INDETERMINATE and block — with no documented rollback to the prior behavior if the new branch causes unexpected operational impact.
An operator needs to revert the change during a tracker-instability incident but finds no back-out procedure in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'If the new hierarchy_incomplete branch causes unexpected claim-gate impact, revert by removing the elif branch and the retry logic, restoring silent-empty-list fallback behavior. Alternatively, gate the new branch behind a feature flag (e.g. ENFORCE_HIERARCHY_COMPLETENESS=true) defaulting to false during initial rollout, enabling operators to opt-in incrementally.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc entry capturing (a) the `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]` + `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` field contract and (b) the fail-closed invariant that an incomplete hierarchy always yields INDETERMINATE.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The plan introduces a new module-level invariant: 'incomplete hierarchy is unconditionally INDETERMINATE, full stop' — a durable architectural decision
New field contract: coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] and coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"] are consumed by finalize_verdict and any future caller
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural decision — a fail-closed hierarchy-completeness invariant and a new `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"]` / `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` field contract — but names no update to an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc to capture this decision durably.
findings.location: Remediation §1–§4 / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new coverage-flag branch and doesn't realise the fail-closed posture is a deliberate invariant, softening it without understanding the rationale.
A new consumer of the coverage dict introduces a flag that collides with or misinterprets hierarchy_incomplete_detail without a schema reference.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to AGENTS.md (or a new ADR file) stating: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] (bool) and coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"] (list[str]) are set by the orchestrator when child-list or child-detail reads do not complete after bounded retries. finalize_verdict treats this as unconditionally INDETERMINATE — no relevance exception exists. This mirrors the llm_unavailable and verify_failed sibling flags.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safe accumulation strategy for `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` — e.g. collect per-child results into a local list before merging into the shared `coverage` dict under a lock, or accumulate via `asyncio`-safe means — and state whether child detail loads run sequentially or in parallel so the implementer can apply the correct primitive.
evidence: Plan: 'record which specific reads failed or were omitted (e.g. coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: list[str] — child ids or "enumeration" for a total failure)'

Plan separately names 'child enumeration' and 'per-child detail loading' as distinct failure modes, implying per-child operations may run concurrently (consistent with orchestrator patterns that fan out child fetches).
No synchronization primitive (lock, queue, thread-safe accumulator) is mentioned for writes to coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"].
findings.finding: The plan introduces `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` as a list that accumulates the ids of failed child reads, but does not specify how concurrent per-child fetch tasks safely write to this shared mutable list. If child detail loading runs in parallel (the plan separates 'child enumeration' from 'per-child detail loading' as distinct operations and applies bounded retries per child, implying parallel execution is possible), multiple tasks racing to append to the same list without a lock or other synchronization primitive create a TOCTOU / data-race: entries can be lost or the list corrupted under concurrent access.
findings.location: Remediation §2 / AC bullet 5: `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: list[str]` — child ids or 'enumeration' for a total failure
scenarios: Any concurrent execution path (e.g. asyncio gather or ThreadPoolExecutor fan-out over children): two child-fetch failures occurring in the same scheduling quantum both attempt list.append on the shared coverage dict value; without a lock, one append can be silently dropped, causing the INDETERMINATE detail to under-report failures — an operator following up would see an incomplete list and potentially miss a broken child.
findings.suggested_fix: If child fetches are fanned out (async gather or thread pool), collect failure ids into task-local lists and merge into `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]` in a single write after all tasks complete, or protect the append with a threading.Lock / asyncio.Lock. State this ownership rule explicitly in the remediation step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all callers that consume the plan-review verdict (or assert that claim-gate is the only one) and confirm each handles the new `INDETERMINATE` output from hierarchy-incomplete cases without regression.
evidence: 'Cascade flag: tightening failure behavior can affect claim-gate availability during transient tracker failures' — only one downstream caller mentioned
Old behavior: finalize_verdict returns PASS for incomplete-hierarchy cases; new behavior: returns INDETERMINATE — a material output-state change for any consumer of the verdict
AC does not include a check that no other caller is broken by the new INDETERMINATE state
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new INDETERMINATE output from finalize_verdict for hierarchy-incomplete cases (replacing the previous silent PASS), but acknowledges only one downstream caller (claim-gate availability) in the Cascade Flag note — other callers that act on a PASS verdict from plan-review (e.g. notification pipelines, dashboard aggregators, or automated promotion gates) are not enumerated or declared absent, leaving it unclear whether the behavior change is safe for all consumers.
findings.location: Cascade flag paragraph / Remediation section
scenarios: A notification pipeline that treats INDETERMINATE as an alerting signal (but previously never saw it for hierarchy-read failures) now fires spuriously during tracker outages
An automated promotion gate that allows PASS but blocks on INDETERMINATE halts deployments during transient tracker issues — a regression not anticipated by the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Caller impact' subsection listing every known consumer of the verdict output and confirming it already handles INDETERMINATE (or will be updated in this ticket), or explicitly assert 'claim-gate is the only consumer' if that is the case — either resolves the acknowledgment gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale statement explaining why the coverage-flag + `finalize_verdict` branch approach is the right fit (e.g., 'this follows the existing parallel-coverage-flag pattern, keeps failure state out of the DET-floor routing path, and avoids exception-based control flow across the assembly→verdict boundary').
evidence: ABSENCE: the Remediation section lists four implementation steps but contains no sentence of the form 'this approach is preferred because…' or 'alternative X was rejected because…'
Plan text explains the cascade flag and operational concern but frames it as a consequence, not a rationale for the design choice
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the chosen approach — embedding failure state as a `coverage` dict flag and adding a new `finalize_verdict` branch — is preferred over structurally different alternatives (e.g., raising a typed `HierarchyReadError` from `_assemble_context_uncached` that propagates to the workflow-level error handler and sets `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete']` there; or performing the completeness check at the DET floor entry point where `has_children` is consumed). The plan describes WHAT the mechanism does but not WHY this layering fits better than alternatives.
findings.location: Remediation section — no 'why this approach' rationale present
scenarios: An implementer uncertain between two viable approaches has no design rationale to resolve the ambiguity, leading to a re-architecture decision during implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` value vocabulary (the `"enumeration"` sentinel and the child-ID list convention) with the coverage/schema definition site (e.g. as a module-level constant `HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_ENUMERATION_SENTINEL = "enumeration"` in orchestrator.py, referenced from both the implementation and the AC), rather than leaving it defined only in scattered prose.
evidence: Remediation step 2 defines the field inline in prose: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: list[str] — child ids or "enumeration" for a total failure'
AC item 5 repeats it: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: child ids, or "enumeration" for a total child-list failure'
det_floor.py PlanContext dataclass (lines 103-138) and orchestrator.py coverage dict have no co-located schema entry for this field or its value vocabulary
The sentinel value 'enumeration' is a string constant with specific semantics — whether it's a literal, a symbolic constant, or a convention is never formalized
findings.finding: The value vocabulary for `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete_detail']` — specifically the reserved sentinel string `"enumeration"` for a total child-list failure vs. child ticket IDs for per-child failures — is defined only in inline prose across two locations (Remediation step 2 and AC item 5) and is never co-located with the `PlanContext`/`coverage` schema definition. The processing rule ('use "enumeration" for total failure, child ids for per-child failure') is a decision rule that belongs alongside the schema, not scattered across prose.
findings.location: Remediation step 2: 'coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"]: list[str] — child ids or "enumeration" for a total failure'; AC item 5
scenarios: An implementer writing the fallback path for total enumeration failure uses 'child_list_failed' or ['enumeration_failed'] instead of ['enumeration'], producing a detail field that AC5 tests won't match.
A downstream consumer (operator tooling, test assertions) parsing hierarchy_incomplete_detail cannot distinguish sentinel from a child ID named 'enumeration' without out-of-band knowledge.

findings.finding: The AC4 relevance-exception can be satisfied vacuously because a failed child enumeration flips `has_children` to False, which changes routing and suppresses all container-specific criteria (P5, G3, G4, decomp-shape) — so 'every criterion being decided' trivially excludes the criteria that SHOULD have run. The plan requires a proof that omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided, but does not require the proof to account for criteria that were never dispatched due to the routing change caused by the incomplete hierarchy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC4 to: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion that would have been evaluated had the hierarchy been complete (including criteria suppressed by routing changes caused by the incomplete data).'

findings.finding: The plan proposes a new 'explicit incomplete-context/indeterminate' verdict outcome (AC3, AC5) without acknowledging the system's existing llm_unavailable → INDETERMINATE pathway in finalize_verdict and _degraded_plan_review_verdict. There is no specification of whether the new outcome reuses the established coverage.llm_unavailable flag and INDETERMINATE verdict, or introduces a parallel mechanism (e.g., coverage.hierarchy_incomplete). A parallel mechanism would create two divergent INDETERMINATE code paths with different coverage shapes, complicating attestation, the claim gate, and consumers.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria AC3, AC5 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py finalize_verdict (lines 503-580)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete: true` flag alongside (not replacing) the existing patterns, update `finalize_verdict` to treat `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete` the same as `llm_unavailable` for the INDETERMINATE branch, and confirm that all four downstream consumers already read that branch.

findings.finding: The 'deterministic, tested proof that omitted context is irrelevant' (the relevance exception in AC4) is a decision branch whose triggering condition is never defined, making the exception and its test case (AC6) impossible to specify or implement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 and AC6

findings.finding: AC4's 'deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant' is effort-framed and does not specify what observable artifact or mechanism constitutes such a proof, making it unevaluable in-session.
findings.location: AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'

findings.finding: The coverage/PlanContext schema field name for hierarchy-completeness status is never defined, yet the verdict protocol (finalize_verdict) must route on it to prevent a clean PASS when children are missing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 and AC3; orchestrator.py:188 (PlanContext construction), orchestrator.py:503-580 (finalize_verdict)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `children_enumeration_ok: bool` and `children_detail_failures: list[str]` to `PlanContext` (or to the `coverage` dict populated in `_assemble_context_uncached`), and add a branch in `finalize_verdict` that sets verdict='INDETERMINATE' when `coverage.get('children_enumeration_ok') is False`.

findings.finding: The Evidence section cites 'orchestrator.py:8-25' as the location of child-enumeration error handling, but reading that file shows lines 8-25 are the module docstring prose. The actual catch/fallback logic is at lines 173-188 (_assemble_context_uncached). Similarly, 'orchestrator.py:26-49' is cited as where review context is built from the incomplete child set, but those lines are stdlib imports; the actual PlanContext assembly is at lines 188-211.
findings.location: Evidence section — 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:8-25' and 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:26-49'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the Evidence section, update the two citations:
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:173-188` — `except Exception` blocks that substitute `listed = []` and fall back to the summary `c` dict
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:188-211` — `PlanContext(…, children=children, …)` construction from the potentially-incomplete child set

findings.finding: AC4 mandates that the relevance exception IS the only permitted exception ('The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof…'), making the tested proof a mandatory deliverable; AC6 makes testing that exception conditional ('the relevance exception if implemented'), implying it may not be implemented at all. This creates a contradiction: if the exception exists (AC4 says it can), AC6 should require its tests unconditionally.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 vs AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC6's trailing clause with: '…and the relevance-exception path (mandatory test coverage if the exception is shipped).' This preserves optionality on shipping the feature while making tests non-optional once it exists.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
